elements.sent-2411-001: Cain ABSTRACT Here the definitions of nearest neighbor, robustness, concordance, and correlation, all of which feature in (Temple 2023) (henceforth abbreviated (T23)), are adjusted to make them completely mathematical while preserving their significance.
elements.sent-2411-002: A characterization is given of the possible limits of a function of distance matrices as the data matrices from which they are derived acquire more and more columns while their number of rows and the distance defining norm (= coefficient, in the terminology of (T23)) are held fixed.
elements.sent-2411-003: No data contribute to the discussion here, but many examples, with standard norms and data matrices having just a few rows and columns, play an important role.
elements.sent-2411-004: Indeed, small data matrices are displayed showing that robustness, defined either of the two ways, can be zero.
elements.sent-2411-005: INTRODUCTION (T23) compares different mathematical approaches to classification by applying a rich mix of analytical, statistical, geometrical, and probabilistic insights and methods to data sets--some empirical and some computer generated.
elements.sent-2411-006: Although the following commentary on (T23) does not engage with any data, it does adduce many examples.
elements.sent-2411-007: These examples may initially seem too simple and artificial to interest the classification community, but these uncomplicated matrices keep the discussion concrete and vivid and chasten any who might over-believe in the role of mathematics in classification.
elements.sent-2411-008: Distance matrices are still at the heart of the discussion, as are again their robustness (defined two ways), concordance, and correlation.
elements.sent-2411-009: In choosing their formal definitions, we part company with other reasonable possible definitions.
elements.sent-2411-010: Since we do not comment on all of (T23) there remains much for others to discuss.
elements.sent-2411-011: We solicit our readers' pardons for having adopted some verbiage and notations differing from that in (T23).
elements.sent-2411-012: Let $M(n \times k)$ denote the set of all $n \times k$ real matrices, and let $M(n)$ denote the set of all real matrices having $n$ rows and a finite number of columns.
elements.sent-2411-013: Let $0(n \times k)$ denote the member of $M(n \times k)$ whose every entry is zero.
elements.sent-2411-014: In order to define the distance between two members of $M(1)$, which have the same number of columns, we rely on norms.
elements.sent-2411-015: Our word ``norm'' corresponds to (T23)'s word ``coefficient''.) We call a function $N$ defined on $M(1)$ a norm iff (= if and only if) for every $v$ and $w$ in $M(1)$ and every real number $c$:.
elements.sent-2411-016: Though not part of the definition of norm, here are two additional properties the norms we consider will satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-017: When $N$ is a norm ``the open $k$-dimensional $N$-ball of radius $r > 0$ centered at $z \in$ $M(1 \times k)$'' is $\{w \in M(1 \times k)\ :\ N(w - z) < r \}$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Below we shall introduce the $p$-norms, a class of norms $N$ satisfying (i)--(v) with $N(v)$ an explicit formula in the entries of $v$, and we turn to them whenever $v$ is specified numerically and we need to compute its norm.
elements.sent-2411-019: To an $X$ in $M(n \times k)$ with $i$-th row denoted $x(i)$, a norm $N$ associates $D(N,X)$, an $n \times n$ so-called ``distance matrix".
elements.sent-2411-020: The entry in row $i$ and column $j$ of $D(N,X)$ is defined to be $N(x(j) - x(i))$, the distance between $x(j)$ and $x(i)$ determined by $N$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Then the entries of $D(N,X)$ are all nonnegative, are all zero on its diagonal, and it itself is symmetric: $N(x(j) - x(i)) = N((-1)(x(i) - x(j))) = |-1|N(x(i) - x(j))$.
elements.sent-2411-022: By ``constant column'' we mean any member of $M(n \times 1)$ all of whose entries are the same, e.g. $0(n \times 1)$.
elements.sent-2411-023: If $Y$ is obtained from $X$ by adding to its columns constant columns and adjoining to it $j - k > 0$ constant columns so that $Y$ is in $M(n \times j)$ then $D(N,Y) = D(N,X)$ because of (iv).
elements.sent-2411-024: For example let $N$ be a norm on $M(1)$ and let $w \to w'$ be an origin fixing $N$-isometry of $M(1 \times k)$, that is $0(1 \times k)' = 0(1 \times j)$ for some integer $j > 0$ and $N(w') = N(w)$ for all $w \in M(1 \times k)$.
elements.sent-2411-025: By (Mazur and Ulam, 1932) this isometry is linear.
elements.sent-2411-026: Then $D(N,Y) = D(N,X)$ if $Y \in M(n \times j)$ and its $i$-th row $y(i) = x(i)'$, because $N(y(j) - y(i)) = N(((x(j) - x(i))') = N(x(j) - x(i))$.
elements.sent-2411-027: If another norm $M$ is involved in the discussion one may ask that $D(M,Y) = D(M,X)$ also.) When constructing examples of distance matrices, it may be helpful to keep in mind that if $Y$ is $X$ with its $i$-th row removed then $D(N,Y)$ is $D(N,X)$ with its $i$-th row and column removed.
elements.sent-2411-028: Furthermore, if $P$ in $M(n \times n)$ is a permutation matrix, i.e. is obtained from the identity matrix of order n by shuffling its rows, then $PX$ is the same shuffle of the rows of $X$ and $D(N,PX) = PD(N,X)P^{T}$ where $T$ stands for ``transpose''.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this way entries in $D(N,X)$ can be repositioned by starting with $PX$ instead of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Let $h(D_{1 , ..., D_{m )$ be a real valued function of $m$ distance-matrix variables $D_{i}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: For $ i = 1, \dots, m$ let $N_{i}$ be a norm.
elements.sent-2411-032: Consider the real-valued function of $m$ variables $X_{i}$ in $M(n)$ given by \begin{equation}H(X_{1}, \dots, X_{m}) = h(D(N_{1},X_{1}), \dots, D(N_{m},X_{m})).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: The apparent greater generality obtained by allowing the $X_{i}$ to have differing numbers of columns is illusory: Just adjoin to each some constant columns e.g. copies of $0(n \times 1)$ to endow them all with the same number of columns.
elements.sent-2411-034: So we may and will assume without loss that each $X_{i}$ is in $M(n \times k)$ for some $k$.
elements.sent-2411-035: What about the limit of $H$ as $k$ approaches $\infty$? If $H$ is a constant function, then the limit is that constant.

elements.sent-2411-037: As we explained above, adjoining $j$ zero columns to each $W_{i}$ to get $V_{i}$ in $M(n \times (j + k))$ will leave the value of $H$ unaltered: $b = H(V_{1 , \dots, V_{m )$.}}

elements.sent-2411-039: Hence, $H$ is not approaching $a$ as the number of columns increases.
elements.sent-2411-040: Thus: THEOREM: $H$ has a limit as the number of columns increases iff $H$ is constant.. REMARKS.
elements.sent-2411-041: We have held $n$ constant while increasing the number of columns.
elements.sent-2411-042: This seems consistent with (T23) and indeed, though (T23) experiments with different fixed values of $n$, there is no discussion of how $n$ and $k$ might move together, perhaps in some kind of coordination, as $k$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-043: So we conclude that (T23) had in mind an arbitrary positive fixed $n$ as $k \to \infty$ that is, $k$ approaches $\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-044: This theorem challenges the remarks in Table 3 of (T23) which offer conclusions about limits as $k \to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-045: To escape this disappointing theorem, we try taking limits of $H$ along sequences.
elements.sent-2411-046: Let $M(n)^{m=}$ denote the set of $m$-tuples of members of $M(n)$, every entry in each $m$-tuple having the same number of columns.
elements.sent-2411-047: A sequence in $M(n)^{m=}$ will be called ``strictly increasing'' iff the common number of columns in a member of the sequence is strictly less than the common number in the next sequence member.
elements.sent-2411-048: Evaluate $H$ at each member of the strictly increasing sequence and try to take the limit of the resulting sequence of values of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-049: A modest gain results: THEOREM: The collection of limits of $H$ along strictly increasing sequences is the topological closure of the range of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-050: On the other hand, if $a$ is a limit point of the range of $H$, there is a sequence in $M(n)^{m}$ and the sequence of values $H$ takes along this sequence converges to $a$.
elements.sent-2411-051: By adjoining zero columns as required to the $m$ matrices in each member of the sequence we can produce a new $m$-tuple each of whose members has the same number of columns and at which $H$ takes the same value it took at the original $m$-tuple.
elements.sent-2411-052: Adjoining additional zero columns if necessary we can produce a strictly increasing sequence along which $H$ takes the same sequence of values it took along the original sequence.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus $a$ is a limit of $H$ along a strictly increasing sequence in $M(n)^{m=}$.

elements.sent-2411-068: NEAREST NEIGHBORS As above $X$ is in $M(n \times k)$ and its $i$-th row is $x(i)$ and $N$ is a norm on $M(1)$ satisfying (iv) and (v).
elements.sent-2411-069: We now define ``$u$ is an $N$-nearest neighbor of $v$'', but only when both $u$ and $v$ are rows of an $X$ in $M(n \times k)$.
elements.sent-2411-070: When $n = 1$ then $D(N,X) = [0]$ and we say: $x(1) = X$ has no nearest neighbor.
elements.sent-2411-071: When $n > 1$ and $i$ and $j$ are unequal members of $\{1, ..., n\}$ we say: row $x(j)$ is an ``$N$-nearest neighbor'' of row $x(i)$ iff $N(x(j) - x(i))$ is the smallest off-diagonal entry in row $i$ of $D(N,X)$.
elements.sent-2411-072: There can be ties, so this may happen for more than one value of $j \neq i$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Thus this definition calls $x(j)$ an $N$-nearest neighbor of $x(i)$ (and vice versa too by symmetry).
elements.sent-2411-074: Some may not like this and would have preferred ``smallest positive off-diagonal entry...''.
elements.sent-2411-075: That would lead to no row of $X$ having a nearest neighbor, if $x(1) = \cdots = x(n)$.) EXAMPLES (1) In Ex1, the two rows are $N$-nearest neighbors of each other.
elements.sent-2411-076: Thus row 2 is the $N$-nearest neighbor of row 1 but not vice versa, and rows 2 and 3 are $N$-nearest neighbors of each other.

elements.sent-2411-078: Set $NEAR(N,X,i) = \{j \neq i : x(j)$ is a $N$-nearest neighbor of $x(i)\}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: When $n > 1$, this set contains at least one integer and at most $n - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Let $NEAR(N,X)$ be the sum for $i = 1, \dots, n$ of the $\#NEAR(N,X,i)$, the number of integers in $NEAR(N,X,i)$.
elements.sent-2411-081: From $n > 1$ we have $0 < \#NEAR(N,X,i) < n$ which leads to $n \leq NEAR(N,X) \leq n(n -1)$ .
elements.sent-2411-082: This upper bound is reached iff all the off-diagonal entries of $D(N,X)$ are the same nonnegative number.
elements.sent-2411-083: When the entries $x_{ij}$ of $X$ satisfy no special relations (some would express this ``$X$ is chosen at random'') then $NEAR(N,X) = n$.
elements.sent-2411-085: Question: What values may $NEAR(N,X)$ assume? ROBUSTNESS Robustness will be defined in terms of nearest neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-086: Throughout this section, we assume $n > 1$ to ensure that we have some nearest neighbors i.e. $NEAR(N,X) > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-087: Let $X' \in$ $M(n \times (k + 1))$ have the same first $k$ columns as $X$ in $M(n \times k)$.
elements.sent-2411-089: Then $NEAR(N,X',i) = \{j \neq i : x'(j)$ is an $N$-nearest neighbor of $x'(i)\}$.
elements.sent-2411-090: In giving a meaning to``robustness'', (T23) says it is the proportion of nearest neighbors that remain unchanged in passing from $X$ to $X'$.
elements.sent-2411-091: This can be interpreted as follows: Let $m(i)$ be the number of $j$'s that are in both $NEAR(N,X,i)$ and $NEAR(N,X',i)$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Then \begin{equation}ROB^{+}(N,X,X') = [m(1) + \cdots + m(n)]/NEAR(N,X)\end{equation} is the proportion which (T23) calls the robustness of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-093: However, we shall call this nonnegative rational quotient ``the $N$-robustness of $X$ with respect to $X'$'', thus emphasizing that it depends on $N$ and $X'$.
elements.sent-2411-095: In fact, it is $< 1$ iff there is at least one $i$ such that $m(i) < \#NEAR(N,X,i)$.
elements.sent-2411-096: If the final column of $X'$ is constant, e.g. all zeros, the distance matrices associated with $X$ and $X'$ are the same since $N$ satisfies (iv) and so the $N$-robustness of $X$ with respect to $X'$ will be $1$, the maximum possible value.
elements.sent-2411-097: Ex6 will show that this robustness can be zero.
elements.sent-2411-098: So, the only possible $N$-robustnesses of $X$ with respect to an $X'$, which is $X$ with one adjoined column, are the fractions \begin{equation}0/NEAR(N,X),\ 1/NEAR(N,X), \dots, NEAR(N,X)/NEAR(N,X),\end{equation} and hence any limit of robustnesses along a sequence of members $X'$ of $M(n \times (k + 1))$ which are one-column extensions of $X$ is also in this set of fractions, and that sequence of fractions must ultimately become constant in order to converge.

elements.sent-2411-199: THE CORRELATION COEFFICIENT OF TWO DISTANCE MATRICES In order to define the correlation between two random variables, we need a sample space $\Omega$ on which they are defined and a probability measure defined on $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-200: One natural choice when working on $n \times n$ distance matrices is \begin{equation}\Omega = \{1, \dots, n\} \times \{1, \dots, n\}\end{equation} with $1/n^{2}$ the measure of each of its points.
elements.sent-2411-201: Since distance matrices are symmetric and zero along their diagonals, another natural choice is \begin{equation}\Omega = T = \{ (i,j) \in \{1, \dots, n\} \times \{1, \dots, n\} : i < j\}\end{equation} with $2/n(n - 1)$ the measure of each of its points.
elements.sent-2411-202: Let $D$ be a distance matrix and $S$ denote the sum of the entries of $D$ which are above its main diagonal.
elements.sent-2411-203: Then the expectation $\mathbb{E (D)$ of $D$ is $2S/n^{2 = (1/n)(2S/n)$ using the first sample space and the easily comparable $2S/n(n-1) = (1/(n - 1))(2S/n)$ using the second.}}
elements.sent-2411-204: We use the first in sequel.
